options.content:
### WHOOP Integration
WHOOP **pushes** recovery data to us (webhook → recent-window re-sync), so nobody is staring at a broken page when ingestion dies — this dashboard is the watcher. The healthy shape: webhooks arrive → syncs succeed → rows land, roughly once per user per morning, plus one token refresh per active hour. Sections: **Webhook ingestion** (deliveries, failures, rejects) · **Syncs & data landed** (attempts and row dispositions — `upserted=0` on green syncs is the classic silent failure) · **Token lifecycle & HTTP surface** (refresh outcomes are the early warning for "my WHOOP stopped syncing"). Dashed yellow / red lines mark warning and critical levels for future alerts. Hover any panel's title (ⓘ) for details; series names match the `whoopsync` structured-log fields, so a spike greps straight to its log lines.

panels.title: Successful syncs (24h)
panels.description: Count of syncs (webhook-triggered window syncs + connect-time backfills) that completed OK in the last 24h. This is the ingestion heartbeat: with any connected user it should be ≥ 1 every morning. ZERO here while the app is healthy means ingestion is dead — webhook registration lapsed, WHOOP-side outage, or every sync failing (check the panels below). Red at 0 by design; this is the number a future 'ingestion dead' alert should watch.

panels.title: Recovery rows upserted (24h)
panels.description: Recovery records actually written in the last 24h. This is the ground truth the dashboard card feeds from — syncs can report OK while writing nothing (the v0.79.x scope bug looked exactly like that from the outside). Yellow at 0: with one connected user a quiet day is possible (late wake, unscored recovery), but a second consecutive zero-day deserves a look at 'Rows by disposition' below.

panels.title: Webhook failures (24h)
panels.description: Deliveries that returned 500 to WHOOP in the last 24h (sync_error, delete_error, route_error). WHOOP retries these 5× over ~an hour and the handler is idempotent, so a lone blip that later succeeds is survivable — but each one has a matching ERROR log line worth reading. Warning at 1, critical at 3 (mirrors the API dashboard's every-5xx-matters stance).

panels.title: Token refresh problems (24h)
panels.description: Refresh attempts in the last 24h that ended invalid_grant (connection flipped to error; the user must reconnect from Settings → Integrations) or persist_error (WHOOP rotated the single-use refresh token but we failed to store the new pair — the connection is likely orphaned). Warning at 1, critical at 2. persist_error specifically always deserves a look at its ERROR log line.

panels.title: Webhook deliveries by outcome
panels.description: Every delivery to POST /webhooks/whoop per trailing 5-minute window, by outcome. Healthy traffic is a morning pulse of `synced` per connected user (WHOOP scores recovery on wake). `unknown_user` means WHOOP still holds a registration for an account we have no connection row for; `not_connected` means events for a revoked/error connection — both are dropped with a 204 on purpose so WHOOP stops retrying. A steady stream of either means a WHOOP-side registration outlived its local connection.

panels.title: Webhook failures & rejects
panels.description: The bad subset: sync_error / delete_error / route_error returned 500 to WHOOP (its retry will re-attempt; each has an ERROR log line), while bad_signature / bad_json were rejected before doing any work. bad_signature is the one to watch separately — more than a stray hit means either the client secret rotated out from under the webhook config, or someone is probing the public endpoint. Dashed yellow / red lines mark the warning (1) and critical (3) thresholds a future alert should use.

panels.title: Sync attempts by kind & result
panels.description: Every sync run per trailing 5-minute window. `window/ok` is the webhook-driven steady state; `backfill/*` appears once per connect (30-day pull). Errors here while webhooks ARE arriving point at the WHOOP data API or our token handling — scope problems, 401s, rate limits — not at delivery; each error carries the sync's wrapped error chain in an ERROR log line. Dashed yellow / red lines mark the warning (1) and critical (3) levels for error-series alerts.

panels.title: Recovery rows by disposition
panels.description: What each sync did with the records it fetched, per trailing 1-hour window. `upserted` is data landing (a connect-time backfill shows as a burst of ~30). The skipped_* series are data-quality signals, not errors: skipped_unscored = WHOOP hasn't scored the recovery yet (a later webhook brings it back), skipped_no_cycle = the recovery's cycle fell outside the fetched window, skipped_bad_date = a cycle we couldn't derive a calendar date from (each has a WARN log with the cycle id). A sustained skipped-heavy mix with no upserts is the 'sync green, dashboard empty' failure shape.

panels.title: Token refreshes by result
panels.description: WHOOP access tokens live ~1h and refresh tokens are single-use, so `ok` refreshes are the connection's pulse — roughly one per active user-hour. `invalid_grant` is the transition behind every "my WHOOP stopped syncing" report: the connection flips to error and stays dead until the user reconnects. `persist_error` is rarer and worse: WHOOP already rotated the token but we failed to store the new pair, likely orphaning the connection. Dashed yellow (1) / red (2) lines mark alert levels for the failure series.

panels.title: 5xx on WHOOP routes
panels.description: Server errors on the WHOOP-owned HTTP surface (OAuth connect/callback, connection status, recovery reads, and the webhook endpoint) per trailing 5-minute window — the user-facing failures, as opposed to the background ingestion the rest of this dashboard watches. A 500 on /auth/whoop/callback is a user watching their connect attempt die. Every envelope's request_id joins to its ERROR log line. Dashed yellow / red lines mark the warning (1) and critical (3) thresholds.

panels.title: WHOOP route traffic (by route)
panels.description: Request rate across the WHOOP HTTP surface. The webhook route is WHOOP calling us (its cadence should match 'Webhook deliveries' above); /me/whoop/connection is the Settings page polling status; /whoop/recovery is the dashboard card and the agent's MCP tool reading data; the /auth/whoop/* pair should appear only when someone connects or reconnects — a burst there outside a known connect is worth a look.
